coredata.dc:title: Characterization of cellulose-derived carbon dots/polyvinyl alcohol films and their application in blueberry preservation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Li
Zheng, Zhongzhao
Fang, Lulu
Zhou, Jianlang
Zheng, Xiaofeng
Gao, Qiujie
Xu, Changyan
coredata.dc:description: A cellulose-derived CDs/PVA composite film was prepared as a multifunctional food packaging material. CDs were synthesized by a one-pot water heating method, with a size distribution ranging from 1.05 to 6.65 nm, and were incorporated into the PVA matrix at concentrations between 0.2 % and 0.8 % to create the nano-composite film. SEM images confirmed the compatibility and potential hydrogen bonding between the low-concentration CDs (<0.4 wt%) and the matrix. This interaction improves the mechanical strength of the composite films. Optical analysis of CDs/PVA films showing good UV shielding effect. Thermal characteristics showed that moderate CDs doping (0.4 wt%) reduced the T g of PVA to 64.1 °C and increased the carbonization rate to 8.49 % at 500 °C. The film with 0.6 wt% CDs demonstrated exceptional DPPH/ABTS scavenging rates of 61.56 % and 97.07 %, along with antibacterial efficiencies of 85 % against E. coli and 87 % against S. aureus. In the blueberry storage test, this film inhibited 10 % spoilage and 10 % weight loss within 8 days. Safety assessment test confirms that the total migration of the film is <10 mg/dm2. The nanocomposite films we prepared provide a sustainable bio-based solution for food packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose-derived CDs
Composite films
UV light blocking
Blueberry preservation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Particle size distribution histogram, (b) TEM, (c) XRD, and (d) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs.
SEM images of the surface (a-e) and cross-section (a1-e1) of the films (respectively: PVA, 0.2 % CDs/PVA, 0.4 % CDs/PVA, 0.6 % CDs/PVA, 0.8 % CDs/PVA).
(a) Stress-strain curve, (b) TS and EB curves, and (c) thickness of films.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (b) transmittance spectrum of prepared films.
(a) TGA curve, (b) DTG curves, and DSC curves of films.
E. coli and S. aureus inhibition rate of different composite films.
Overall migration values of different film samples in food simulants.
Effects of different films on the preservation of blueberries. (a) Decay rate, and (b) weight loss.
Surface color and transmittance of different composite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All values expressed as means ± standard deviations. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Different letters “a, b, c, d, e, f” in the same column indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic parameters of CD/PVA hydrogels with different CDs contents.
Effects of film solutions on the growth of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus.

head.ce:title: Characterization of cellulose-derived carbon dots/polyvinyl alcohol films and their application in blueberry preservation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A cellulose-derived CDs/PVA composite film was prepared as a multifunctional food packaging material. CDs were synthesized by a one-pot water heating method, with a size distribution ranging from 1.05 to 6.65 nm, and were incorporated into the PVA matrix at concentrations between 0.2 % and 0.8 % to create the nano-composite film. SEM images confirmed the compatibility and potential hydrogen bonding between the low-concentration CDs (<0.4 wt%) and the matrix. This interaction improves the mechanical strength of the composite films. Optical analysis of CDs/PVA films showing good UV shielding effect. Thermal characteristics showed that moderate CDs doping (0.4 wt%) reduced the T g of PVA to 64.1 °C and increased the carbonization rate to 8.49 % at 500 °C. The film with 0.6 wt% CDs demonstrated exceptional DPPH/ABTS scavenging rates of 61.56 % and 97.07 %, along with antibacterial efficiencies of 85 % against E. coli and 87 % against S. aureus. In the blueberry storage test, this film inhibited 10 % spoilage and 10 % weight loss within 8 days. Safety assessment test confirms that the total migration of the film is <10 mg/dm2. The nanocomposite films we prepared provide a sustainable bio-based solution for food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging is crucial for ensuring food safety and maintaining food quality throughout processing and manufacturing to transportation, storage, and consumer access [1]. Traditional petroleum-based plastics, including polyethylene, polyethylene terephthalate, polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene and polypropylene, have been widely used in food packaging systems due to their mechanical strength, barrier properties against pollutants and logistics convenience [2,3]. However, these materials possess persistent environmental recalcitrance because they are difficult to biodegrade and break down into microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs). These particles not only infiltrate ecosystems, causing long-term ecological damage, but also pose a bioaccumulation risk in human tissues through ingestion or inhalation [4–6]. Therefore, developing biodegradable, biocompatible, and low-toxicity biopolymer alternatives has become an important and urgent task to mitigate public health hazards and environmental degradation.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), a hydrophilic, biodegradable and highly biocompatible polymer [7]. It is recognized as a cost-effective and environmentally friendly packaging material for a wide range of packaging, biomedical, food and clinical applications [8,9]. However, its poor thermal stability, high light transmittance, and excessive water solubility, which cannot meet the basic requirements of food packaging, greatly limit its practical applications. To address these limitations, the incorporation of functional nanomaterials into PVA films has become one of the most effective strategies to improve their performance [10,11]. Notably, the abundant hydrogen bonding groups in the PVA matrix can facilitate the interaction with various active nanomaterials, resulting in the formation of novel hydrogen bonding networks and thus improving their functional properties [12].

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with dimensions ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm [13], exhibit outstanding biocompatibility [14] low toxicity [15], and antimicrobial activity [16], making them promising candidates for applications in biomedicine [17], nanovaccine [18], drug delivery [19], and nano-antibacterial agents [20,21]. Owing to their exceptional stability, CDs possess remarkable free radical scavenging capabilities, effectively reducing oxidative stress responses and mitigating oxidative damage to cells and tissues, thereby contributing to the maintenance of redox homeostasis within biological systems. Furthermore, CDs have demonstrated superior antimicrobial properties [22,23]. On the one hand, their high specific surface area and abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface can damage the bacterial cell membrane through physical contact, causing mechanical damage to the bacterial membrane structure; on the other hand, CDs can induce the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the microorganisms, triggering an oxidative stress reaction and leading to DNA damage and metabolic disorders [24]. Extensive studies have confirmed the non-cytotoxicity of CDs [25], providing robust evidence for their expanded utilization in food packaging. For instance, Du et al. [26] developed a composite film using PVA and zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CQDs) to enhance the preservation of mangoes. The results revealed that the composite film significantly extended shelf life, offering a novel research direction in food preservation through the integration of renewable resources and nanotechnology.

In this study, cellulose was used as the precursor to prepare green and non-toxic CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized CDs were doped into the PVA matrix at different proportions as nano-fillers to prepare CDs/PVA composite membranes. The mechanical properties, water-blocking properties, antioxidant properties, and antibacterial properties of the CDs/PVA composite membranes were studied. The composite membranes were applied to the preservation of blueberries, and the feasibility of this film in fruit packaging was explored to provide a reference for the development of new food packaging materials.

Cellulose was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). PVA (1799, alcoholysis degree of 98–99 %) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial (Shanghai, China). Nutrient agar was purchased from Haibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. of Qingdao High-tech Industrial Park (Qingdao, China). 2,2-diphenyl-1-pyridine hydrazide (DPPH) and ABTS were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 6633) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Sciences Institute of Microbiology. Fresh blueberries were purchased from a supermarket in Nanjing, China.

The CDs were synthesized by the typical hydrothermal method and were slightly modified [27]. A mixture of 0.5 g of cellulose and 60 mL of deionized water was placed into a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reaction vessel and subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous microporous filter membrane, and the filtrate was dried to obtain a viscous solid. This viscous solid was completely dissolved in deionized water, filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous microporous filter membrane to obtain the filtrate, left to stand at room temperature for 3 days, and then freeze-dried to obtain the solid material, which was the cellulose-derived CDs.

PVA (10 g) was dissolved in 90 mL of deionized water and stirred with a glass rod for 10 min. The mixture was then placed in a water bath at 90 °C for 1 h to obtain a 10 % PVA solution. Different concentrations of CDs (0.2 %, 0.4 %, 0.6 %, and 0.8 %) were added to the PVA solution, stirred at room temperature for 1 h, and then the mixture was evenly poured into a glass petri dish and dried at 60 ± 2 °C for 6 h to obtain CDs/PVA films with different concentrations. Depending on the concentration of added CDs, the composite films were named PVA, 0.2 % CDs/PVA, 0.4 % CDs/PVA, 0.6 % CDs/PVA and 0.8 % CDs/PVA.

The morphology and structure of the CDs were examined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL-200, thermal field emission electron microscope). The surface morphology and cross-sectional morphology of the composite film were observed using a scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Sigma300 + Oxford Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer, ULTIM MAX 40). The X-ray diffraction pattern of the CDs was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (AXIS-Ultra DLD, Shimadzu, Japan) with the scanning angle ranging from 5° to 60°. The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Fukuoka, Japan). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the films were recorded using a Nicolet IS10 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with a wavenumber range of 500–4000 cm−1.

The mechanical performance experiments were carried out as per the method of Zhao et al. [28], with some modification. The thickness of the films was measured using a micrometer (KEYENCE, CL-3000, Japan). Six random positions on the same composite film were selected, and the average value was taken as the thickness of the film. The mechanical properties of the composite films were tested using a universal testing machine (UTM4204X) to determine the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB). The films were cut into rectangular specimens of 150 mm × 15 mm, and each type of film was tested five times with the average value reported. TS and EB values were directly collected from the output data.

The absorption spectrum and transmission spectrum of films were analyzed by a ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, USA) to evaluate the UV blocking properties. According to the method of Ding et al [29], with slight modifications, the films were cut into 1 × 4 cm strips for full wavelength scanning from 200 to 600 nm. The perceived hue of the films was evaluated using a colorimeter (LS173, Technology Co., Ltd. Shenzhen, China), with a white standard plate [brightness (L ⁎) = 97.29, reddishness (a ⁎) = −0.42, yellowishness (b ⁎) = −0.74] as the reference [30]. The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
ΔE =
L ∗
−
L 0 ∗
2
+
a ∗
−
a 0 ∗
2
+
b ∗
−
b 0 ∗
2
where L ⁎, a ⁎ and b ⁎ represent the color parameters of the film samples. Each film was measured at five different locations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor transmission rate and water contact angle
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the method of Sun et al [31], with slight modifications, the Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) was measured at a specific temperature (38 °C) and humidity (90 %) using a WVTR-C1 water vapor transmission rate tester (Jinan Sk Test Technology Co., Ltd.). The average values of different films were obtained by averaging the results of three repeated experiments. The WVTR was calculated using the following formula (2):
(2)
WVTR = G / At
where: G is the total amount of water vapor transmitted through the film during the test period, A is the effective area of the film and t is the test duration.
The water contact angle (WCA) of the films was measured using a contact angle measuring instrument (FCA2000A2, China).

The thermal stability of films was performed using a thermogravimetric analysis instrument (TGA 209, NETZSCH) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC204, NETZSCH). TG experiments were tested from 30 to 550 °C in heating rate 10 °C /min−1and shielding gas is nitrogen (N2) at gas flow 30 mL/min−1. DSC was performed from the room temperature to 350 °C in heating rate 10 °C /min [32].

The antioxidant activity was further evaluated using the spectrophotometric method with a minor modification [33]. 20 mg of the film sample was added to 3 mL of DPPH ethanol solution (0.06 mM) and reacted under room temperature and dark conditions for 30 min, followed by the measurement of absorbance at 517 nm. The DPPH free radical scavenging capacity was calculated using Eq. (2):
(3)
DPPH scavenging capacity
%
=
1 −
A i
/
A 0
× 100 %
where A
0 represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution and A
i represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution containing the sample.

For ABTS analysis was performed according to Roy et al. [34] 2.45 mM of potassium sulfate was mixed with 7 mM of ABTS solution and t incubating overnight in the dark. 4 mL of ABTS assay solution was mixed with 20 mg of the film, reacted under room temperature and dark conditions for 30 min, and the absorbance was measured at 734 nm. The ABTS free radical scavenging capacity was calculated using Eq. (3):
(4)
ABTS scavenging capacity
%
=
1 −
A i
/
A 0
× 100 %
where A
0 represents the absorbance of the ABTS solution and A
i represents the absorbance of the ABTS solution containing the sample.

The antibacterial activities of the samples were evaluated using the colony count method [33]. E. coli and S. aureus were used as representative bacteria for testing. Both purchased strains of E. coli and S. aureus were activated by shaking at 37 °C, then diluted by factors of one million and one hundred thousand, respectively. After dilution, 0.8 mL of each bacterial suspension was taken and mixed with 1.8 mL of film solutions of different concentrations (PVA, 0.2 % CDs/PVA, and 0.6 % CDs/PVA) and spread onto plastic petri dishes containing nutrient agar. The dishes were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, after which the colonies were photographed and recorded. The rate of bacterial growth inhibition was calculated using the following Eq. (5) [30],
(5)
Bacteriostatic Rate
%
=
N 0
−
N i
/
N 0
where N
0 and N
i are the number of the colonies of the control group and the tested samples, respectively.

According to the method proposed by Roy et al. [35] to evaluate the safety of composite films in the application process. Distilled water, 3 % w/v acetic acid, 50 % v/v ethanol, and n-heptane were used to simulate aqueous, acidic, alcoholic, and fatty food types, respectively. Film samples with uniform dimensions were immersed in equal volumes of the food simulants. The film samples were kept in distilled water, 3 % acetic acid, and 50 % ethanol at 40 °C for 10 days, while the film samples in n-heptane were incubated at 38 °C for 30 min. At the end of the exposure, the simulants were evaporated and the extracted migration substances were measured using gravimetric methods. The overall migration (OM) was expressed as the mass of total components released per square decimeter of contact surface (mg/dm2).

Following previously reported methods [36], five experimental groups were set up with an experimental temperature of 25 °C. For each group, five fresh blueberries of similar weight and size were selected and placed in a 25 mL beaker. Group 1 served as the control group, with an 80 × 80 mm piece of polyethylene (PE) film used to seal the mouth of the beaker. Group 2 was the PVA group, sealed with an 80 × 80 mm piece of PVA film. Groups 3–5 were the CDs/PVA composite film groups with different concentration ratios of CDs (0.2 %, 0.4 %, and 0.6 %, respectively). The experimental period lasted for 8 days, during which the quality indicators of the blueberries in these five groups were measured at the same time each day to study the impact of the five types of composite films on the quality changes of the blueberries.
The decay rate was determined by visual observation and calculated by dividing the number of decayed blueberries by the total number of initial blueberries.
The weight loss of blueberries was calculated by dividing the weight of the blueberry sample lost by the initial weight. Each test was repeated five times.

All experiments were repeated three times. The data were shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The significance of the experimental data was analyzed (p < 0.05) with Duncan's multiple range test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using the SPSS Statistics 26.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

Fig. 1(a) presents a particle size histogram indicating that the diameters of the CDs range from 1.05 to 6.65 nm. TEM image shown in Fig. 1(b) reveals that the prepared CDs are approximately spherical in shape and exhibit good dispersion without aggregation. The XRD pattern of the CDs, depicted in Fig. 1(c), displays a pronounced diffraction peak at 21.21°, corresponding to the [002] plane of graphitic carbon [37]. Fig. 1(d) illustrates the emission spectra of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 to 390 nm. The CDs demonstrate significant excitation independence, with the fluorescence intensity initially increasing and then decreasing with the extension of the excitation wavelength, indicating a redshift phenomenon. Moreover, the strongest fluorescence intensity of the CDs is observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 437 nm.

The effect of CDs doping on the microstructure of the composite films was investigated, and the surface and cross-sectional morphology of pure PVA films as well as CDs/PVA films with different amounts of carbon dot doping were observed by SEM (Fig. 2 ). The dense surface and homogeneous structure of the pure PVA films (Fig. 2a, a1) indicate their excellent film-forming ability. The surface roughness of the composite films gradually increased with the increase of carbon dot doping. At 0.2 wt% of carbon dots (Fig. 2b, b1), the films maintained the original structural homogeneity, and only a small amount of granularity was observed on the surface, indicating that the CDs were effectively dispersed at a low concentration [38]. However, at 0.4 wt% CDs content (Fig. 2c, c1), obvious particle aggregation appeared on the surface, and the cross-section showed tiny pore-like structures. Accompanied by the increase of carbon dot content to 0.6 wt% and 0.8 wt% (Fig. 2d/e, d1/e1), the surface showed severe aggregation of nanoparticles and the cross-section showed a porous network, indicating that the structure became loose. These morphological changes likely originated from the poor dispersion of higher concentrations of CDs in PVA, resulting in the formation of nanoclusters, which disrupted the structural continuity of the PVA matrix and consequently affected the properties of the composite films. Overall, proper doping of carbon dots is beneficial to improve the structural homogeneity of the films, while over-doping leads to structural disruption and functional degradation of the films.

The structure and surface state of the prepared CDs were analyzed using FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 3 , the spectrum of the CDs exhibited strong symmetric stretching vibrations of CO (C-O-C group) and CO, OH stretching vibration peaks at 1023 cm−1, 2929 cm−1, and 3197 cm−1, indicating the presence of a certain amount of epoxy groups, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs [28]. When comparing composite films with different concentrations of CDs, the absorption peaks of the CDs/PVA composite films were very similar to those of the PVA film, which may be due to the low content of CDs in the PVA film overshadowing the characteristic absorption peaks of the CDs. In the PVA film, the OH stretching vibration peak appeared at 3263 cm−1, and with the increase in the content of CDs, this peak slightly decreased in the CDs/PVA film, suggesting the formation of new hydrogen bonds between PVA and CDs [39].

Fig. 4 illustrates the effect of CDs concentration on the mechanical properties of the composites. Fig. 4(c) shows that the thickness of the films gradually increases with the increase of CDs content, which is related to the enhancement of intermolecular interactions and the dispersion of nanofillers in the PVA matrix. The stress-strain curves (a) show that the pure PVA films exhibit typical elastic-plastic behavior with good flexibility. Upon addition of CDs, the composite films at low concentration (0.2 %) exhibited higher tensile stresses, suggesting that the homogeneous dispersion of CDs enhanced the mechanical properties of the films. However, when the concentration of CDs was further increased to 0.6 %–0.8 %, the tendency of tensile strength of the composite films decreased, suggesting that the high concentration of CDs may aggregate within the matrix, which reduces the load-bearing capacity of the films. The TS and EB curves in Fig. 4(b) further confirm the above phenomenon: at a low concentration of CDs (0.2 wt%), the TS of the composite film is significantly higher than that of the pure PVA, which indicates that there is an interfacial enhancement effect. At the same time, the EB reaches a peak, indicating that the strong hydrogen bonding between the reactive functional groups on the surface of CDs and the PVA matrix enhances the ductility of the films. However, at too high CDs loading (0.6–0.8 wt%), both TS and EB decreased dramatically. This is mainly due to the stress concentration induced by nanoparticle aggregation, which disrupts the continuity and homogeneity of the composite matrix - a phenomenon confirmed in the SEM analysis of Fig. 3 [40].

The UV–vis absorption spectra measurement was applied to assess the UV barrier capacities of different CDs/PVA films. As displayed in Fig. 5(a), pure PVA film exhibits almost no significant absorption in the UV range of 200–300 nm, indicating its inherently weak UV-blocking capability. However, with increasing concentrations of CDs, the absorption intensity of the composite films in the 250–290 nm wavelength range progressively increases, demonstrating excellent UV absorption performance. This enhancement is due to the presence of π-conjugated structures and UV-absorbing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups) in CDs [41]. The transmittance results shown in Fig. 5(b) reveal that pure PVA film maintains high transparency across the entire wavelength range. In contrast, the transmittance of CDs/PVA composite films decreases with increasing CDs concentration, particularly in the UV region, confirming their effective UV-shielding capability. Table 1 provides a visual representation of the films. Pure PVA films exhibited high transparency, while the composite films containing CDs displayed a slight yellow tint, which became more pronounced as the CDs content increased. As shown in Table 1, the L ⁎ value of the CDs/PVA film decreases significantly with the addition of toner, while the a ⁎ and b ⁎ values increase, leading to an increase in the ΔE value.

Fig. 6(a) illustrates the trend of WVTR for composite films with varying concentrations of CDs, reflecting the impact of CDs doping on the barrier properties of the composite films. The WVTR increases with the concentration of carbon dots. PVA itself is a hydrophilic polymer, and cellulose is also rich in hydroxyl groups and highly hydrophilic. Therefore, the introduction of cellulose-based carbon dots may further enhance the hydrophilicity of the composite films, leading to an increase in water absorption and a decrease in water resistance. Meanwhile, the WCA (Fig. 6b) showed an overall decreasing trend, which further proved the decreased water resistance of the composite films.

The thermal properties of the composite films are shown in Fig. 7 and Table 2 . As depicted in Fig. 7(a), the weight retention profiles of composite films exhibit multi-stage decomposition behavior, with corresponding DTG curves presented in Fig. 7(b). All films demonstrated three distinct thermal degradation phases. The initial mass loss (30–150 °C) mainly corresponds to the loss of water and low-molecular-weight compounds. The second weight loss occurred between 150 and 400 °C, which corresponds to the degradation of PVA side chains, leading to the production of a substantial quantity of water and small, combustible molecules, while the residual portion formed more thermally stable conjugated structures [42]. As for CDs/PVA films, functional groups such as -OH on the surface are easily thermally decomposed and eliminated at low temperatures, which leads to lower 5 % thermal degradation temperature (T d 5%) and the maximum pyrolysis temperature (T max) than in pure PVA film. The increase in CDs content leads to an increase in the number of side chains and small molecules in the PVA composite films, which suggests that the CDs promote the thermal degradation of the PVA composite films in advance. The third weight loss stage occurred at 400–500 °C shows that the CDs/PVA films display higher weight loss rates compared to the PVA film, this is attributed to the degradation of CDs structures [43]. A substantial boost in char formation when fie if the concentration of carbon dots is too high, the incompatibility at the interface will affect the formation of residual carbon. The DSC curves of films were shown in Fig. 7(c). All films displayed a sole transition temperature (T g), reflecting their homogeneous nature. Pure PVA had the highest T g at 69.4 °C. Yet, its T g diminished progressively as the CDs content rose, likely stemming from how CDs inclusion heightened the polymer chains' free volume, thus boosting their mobility and causing the T g to drop.

Antioxidant activity is a crucial aspect of food packaging films and plays a significant role in maintaining food quality. Fig. 8 shows the PVA film has almost no scavenging effect on free radicals. However, as the concentration of CDs in the composite film increases, the DPPH scavenging ability gradually improves, and the ABTS scavenging ability increases sharply [44]. When 0.6 % CDs are incorporated, the scavenging rates of the composite film for DPPH and ABTS reach 61.56 % and 97.07 % respectively, showing no significant difference from the scavenging rates at 0.8 % concentration. This indicates that the 0.6 % CDs/PVA film has the highest free - radical scavenging rate. The main difference in the scavenging rates of DPPH and ABTS is due to the hydrophilicity of the PVA film. Compared with the DPPH ethanol solution, the PVA film is more soluble in the ABTS aqueous solution, resulting in a faster release rate of CDs in the ABTS aqueous solution than in the DPPH ethanol solution. The high antioxidant activity of CDs is attributed to the large number of electron - donating groups on its surface, such as hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs greatly enhances the free - radical scavenging effect and endows the composite film with strong antioxidant activity [45].

To simulate real-world food contact scenarios, the migration behavior of the films was evaluated using four food simulants: distilled water (aqueous), 50 % v/v ethanol (alcoholic), 3 % w/v acetic acid (acidic), and n-heptane (fatty). OM values were lowest in distilled water and highest in n-heptane, a trend attributed to the nonpolar nature of n-heptane, which promotes film swelling and structural loosening, thereby accelerating component dissolution [35]. Fig. 10 illustrates that incorporating CDs significantly diminishes migration rates across all simulants, with the 0.6 % CDs/PVA film exhibiting the minimal OM values. Crucially, all films' OM levels remain under the 10 mg/dm2 regulatory limit (Commission regulation, 2011), thus adhering to food packaging safety standards. However, while these in vitro findings confirm initial safety, additional regulatory evaluations, such as toxicity assessments and long-term stability tests, are imperative before commercialization.

The storage quality of blueberries in different composite films was evaluated by visual appearance, decay rate and weight loss rate as shown in Fig. 11 and Table 4 . Blueberries in all groups remained in their initial intact state for the first 2 days. On day 4, the PE and pure PVA film groups showed significant mold, which subsequently worsened. In contrast, blueberries stored in the 0.6 % CDs/PVA composite film maintained structural integrity throughout the study with little to no mold. By day 8, the PE and pure PVA groups had 80 % to 100 % blueberry decay, while the 0.2 % and 0.4 % CDs/PVA film groups had <40 % decay. Notably, the 0.6 % CDs/PVA film inhibited decay to 10 %. Similarly, the weight loss of blueberries in the PE group exceeded 30 %, followed by the pure PVA and low concentration CDs/PVA film groups (20 % to 25 %), while the 0.6 % CDs/PVA film group had the lowest weight loss of 10 %, highlighting its excellent freshness retention. These results indicate that 0.6 % CDs/PVA composite films are the optimal solution for extending shelf life and minimizing spoilage in blueberries.

In this study, PVA was used as a substrate and cellulose-derived CDs were added to fabricate PVA/CDs composite films. The morphology, mechanical, optical, thermal, antimicrobial, antioxidant and freshness preservation properties of the composite films with different CDs contents were characterized. The results showed that the PVA/CDs composite films have excellent mechanical properties, efficient UV shielding properties and enhanced thermal stability. Meanwhile, the composite films were able to resist DPPH and ABTS radicals as well as strong antimicrobial properties. Notably, 0.6 % PVA/CDs films combining physicochemical and functional properties were applied for blueberry preservation. Experimental results showed that the composite film effectively extended the shelf life of blueberries up to 8 days at 25 °C by reducing water loss and inhibiting microbial growth. However, based on the WVTR and WCA results, the composite films faced the limitations of relatively high WVTR and moderate water resistance, which provide potential areas of improvement for future research. In conclusion, the developed PVA/CDs-0.6 % film is promising as an innovative active food packaging material for fruit preservation.

Li Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhongzhao Zheng: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lulu Fang: Validation, Methodology. Jianlang Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Xiaofeng Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Qiujie Gao: Writing – review & editing. Changyan Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase change materials in food packaging: a review
